title: LLM Lineage research corpus
description: A source-backed corpus of the works and ideas needed to explain the development of modern large language models.

orderingPolicy.description: A work is ordered by the explicitly referenced chronology event in chronology.orderingEventId; display dates never infer day precision from month- or year-level records.
orderingPolicy.tieBreak: When ordering events are equal, preserve editorial sequence and disclose concurrency rather than imply priority.

works.title: An Example of Statistical Investigation of the Text Eugene Onegin Concerning the Connection of Samples in Chains
works.displayCitation: A. A. Markov, 1913 Academy lecture and bulletin article; English translation in Science in Context 19(4), 591–600 (2006).

events.label: Presentation to the Academy’s Physical-Mathematical Department

date.note: Date printed on the original and translation; the source does not state the calendar convention.

events.label: Publication in the Imperial Academy bulletin

date.note: The catalog year is preserved without asserting a day- or month-level calendar conversion.

events.label: English translation in Science in Context

dateEvidence.locator: Science in Context volume 19, issue 4, December 2006

events.label: Cambridge online publication of the English translation

versions.label: 1913 Russian lecture and journal text

bibliography.citation: A. A. Markov, “Примѣръ статистическаго изслѣдованія надъ текстомъ „Евгенія Онѣгина“ иллюстрирующій связь испытаній въ цѣпь,” Извѣстія Императорской Академіи Наукъ, series VI, 7(3), 153–162 (1913).

versions.notes: The original journal text is the historical work. The presentation date is printed in the article, while the issue-publication record is only assigned year precision here.

versions.label: 2006 peer-reviewed English translation

bibliography.citation: A. A. Markov, “An Example of Statistical Investigation of the Text Eugene Onegin Concerning the Connection of Samples in Chains,” Science in Context 19(4), 591–600 (December 2006).

versions.notes: The journal issue is dated December 2006 and Cambridge records online publication on 4 January 2007; those dates are stored separately.

inclusion.rationale: The study makes dependence in literary text empirically inspectable while remaining separable from the later probabilistic-language-model spine.

significance.beginner: A short literary sequence can reveal that what comes next depends on what came immediately before.
significance.expert: The study estimates conditional vowel/consonant frequencies and compares observed dispersion with independent, simple-chain, and more complex chain assumptions.

limitations: The alphabet is collapsed to the binary classes vowel and consonant, discarding letter identity, words, syntax, and meaning.
The work does not train a predictor or present text generation as its objective.
Markov explicitly says the empirical example may not fully satisfy the theoretical assumptions.
The underlying Pushkin edition, exact terminal character, and a fully reproducible normalization pipeline are not specified.

uncertainties.note: The reviewed primary and historical sources do not document a direct Markov-to-Shannon influence path.
uncertainties.resolutionCondition: A primary source from Shannon or a participant would need to identify Markov’s text study as an influence.

works.title: A Mathematical Theory of Communication
works.displayCitation: C. E. Shannon, “A Mathematical Theory of Communication,” The Bell System Technical Journal 27(3), 379–423 (July 1948), and 27(4), 623–656 (October 1948).

versions.label: Original two-installment journal article

bibliography.citation: C. E. Shannon, “A Mathematical Theory of Communication,” The Bell System Technical Journal 27(3), 379–423 (July 1948), and 27(4), 623–656 (October 1948).

versions.notes: The title is identical in both issues. Installment labels are editorial conveniences, not part of the published title.

versions.label: Corrected combined reprint

bibliography.citation: C. E. Shannon, “A Mathematical Theory of Communication,” corrected combined reprint of The Bell System Technical Journal 27, 379–423 and 623–656 (1948).

versions.notes: The corrected reprint combines both installments and uses internal pages 1–55; claims retain original BSTJ pagination.

inclusion.rationale: The paper brings discrete stochastic sources, conditional sequence probabilities, entropy, and constructed English approximations together in one inspectable formal account.

significance.beginner: Changing how much earlier text affects the next choice changes both the appearance and predictability of generated symbol sequences.
significance.expert: The paper represents written language as a discrete stochastic source, develops finite-state and n-gram conditionals, and relates longer contexts to conditional source entropy.

limitations: The paper is an engineering theory of communication and explicitly brackets semantic meaning from that engineering problem.
The English sequences are illustrative samples constructed from letter and word frequency information and book-based successor sampling, not learned neural models or evidence of comprehension.
Many discrete-source results use finite-state models and, unless stated otherwise, an ergodicity assumption; these idealizations should not be universalized.

works.title: Prediction and Entropy of Printed English
works.displayCitation: C. E. Shannon, “Prediction and Entropy of Printed English,” The Bell System Technical Journal 30(1), 50–64 (January 1951).

versions.label: Original journal article

bibliography.citation: C. E. Shannon, “Prediction and Entropy of Printed English,” The Bell System Technical Journal 30(1), 50–64 (January 1951).

versions.notes: The issue supplies month precision. The day shown by a later Bell Labs catalog is not used as the ordering date.

inclusion.rationale: The paper turns contextual next-character predictability into an explicit experiment and connects ranked predictions to entropy bounds.

significance.beginner: A guessing experiment makes it possible to feel how preceding text narrows the plausible next character.
significance.expert: The paper estimates conditional character entropy from human rank predictions, separates observed prediction from an ideal conditional-probability ranking, and derives upper and lower bounds.

limitations: The experiments use human next-character prediction, not a trained language model, next-word task, or free-running generator.
The main context experiment draws from one literary source, permits reference aids, and does not report participant demographics or variability.
The long-context estimates use smoothed samples and apply an ideal-predictor lower bound to human rankings with an explicit approximation caveat.

uncertainties.note: The paper’s reported long-context condition and its N versus N-1 indexing should be preserved rather than silently normalized.
uncertainties.resolutionCondition: The interface must show the paper’s labels and a separate normalized explanation.

ideas.name: Text as a dependent sequence
ideas.description: Adjacent symbol classes in observed text can have different conditional frequencies than independence would predict.

uncertainties.note: The opening treatment is intentionally limited to the vowel/consonant abstraction used in the 1913 study.

ideas.name: Language as a conditional stochastic source
ideas.description: A discrete language source emits successive symbols according to probabilities that may depend on preceding choices.

ideas.name: Entropy as sequence uncertainty
ideas.description: Conditional entropy quantifies average uncertainty about a next event when contextual information is known.

ideas.name: Increasing-order language approximation
ideas.description: Successively longer conditional contexts can produce samples with more of a language’s local statistical structure.

uncertainties.note: Order labels in the paper are preserved alongside explicit context length because they differ from some modern conventions.

ideas.name: Contextual next-symbol prediction
ideas.description: The predictability of the next character can be studied while the correct preceding text remains visible.

ideas.name: Prediction-based entropy bounds
ideas.description: The rank frequencies of next-character predictions can be related to upper and lower bounds on conditional entropy.

sources.title: Publisher record for Prediction and Entropy of Printed English
sources.accessNote: Publisher metadata gives volume 30, issue 1, January 1951, pp. 50–64.

sources.title: Nokia Bell Labs publication record
sources.accessNote: Official Bell Labs catalog page; its display day is not used to add day precision to the journal issue date.

sources.title: Readable facsimile of Prediction and Entropy of Printed English
sources.accessNote: Open facsimile retaining printed pages 50–64; printed page numbers, not PDF indices, are used in claim locators.

claims.statement: Markov’s sample contains 20,000 Russian letters from the specified opening span of Eugene Onegin, with each retained letter classified as a vowel or consonant.

evidence.support: States the 20,000-letter span, exclusions, and binary vowel/consonant treatment.
Provides the peer-reviewed English rendering of the sample scope and classification.

limitations: The hard and soft signs are excluded, and the source does not fully specify a modern reproducible normalization pipeline.

claims.statement: The study defines conditional vowel probabilities for one- and two-predecessor classes, reports both one-predecessor estimates, and uses selected two-predecessor estimates—p₁,₁ ≈ 0.104 (115/1104) and q₀,₀ ≈ 0.132 (505/3827)—in its more complex-chain calculation.

locator.label: Original pp. 153–154
locator.paragraph: Definitions of p, p1, p0, and two-predecessor quantities

evidence.support: Defines the conditional quantities to be estimated from the observed text.

locator.label: English translation p. 591
locator.paragraph: Definitions of p, p1, p0, and two-predecessor quantities

evidence.support: Names the one- and two-predecessor conditional probabilities in English.

locator.label: Original pp. 158–160
locator.paragraph: Reported one-predecessor rates and selected two-predecessor estimates

evidence.support: Reports p1 and p0, then p₁,₁ = 115/1104 and q₀,₀ = 505/3827 for the more complex-chain calculation.

locator.label: English translation pp. 596–598
locator.paragraph: Reported one-predecessor rates and selected two-predecessor estimates

evidence.support: Provides the English rendering of the one-predecessor rates and the two selected numerical two-predecessor estimates.

limitations: The states are only vowel and consonant classes.

claims.statement: In the observed sample, the estimated vowel rate is about 0.128 after a vowel and about 0.663 after a consonant, so it changes substantially with the preceding class.

evidence.support: Reports 1104/8638 and 7534/11361 and interprets the change by predecessor class.
Translates the quantitative comparison and its stated dependence on the preceding letter class.

limitations: The denominator differs by one at the sequence boundary and the result describes this sample, not every Russian text.

claims.statement: Markov compares an observed dispersion coefficient of about 0.208 with 1 for independence, about 0.3 for the simple-chain calculation, and about 0.195 for the more complex-chain calculation.

locator.label: Original pp. 157–160
locator.paragraph: Observed, independent, simple-chain, and complex-chain dispersion comparison

evidence.support: Gives the observed and theoretical coefficients and compares their agreement.

locator.label: English translation pp. 595–598
locator.paragraph: Observed, independent, simple-chain, and complex-chain dispersion comparison

evidence.support: Provides the English rendering of the comparison and qualification.

limitations: The historical dispersion calculation is not a modern p-value or an exact model-identification test.

claims.statement: After comparing observed and theoretical dispersion, Markov cautions that the example may not fully satisfy all of the theoretical assumptions.

evidence.support: Explicitly limits the strength of the empirical model-fit conclusion.
Supplies the English wording of the explicit qualification.

claims.statement: Shannon represents a discrete information source as a stochastic process that emits successive symbols with probabilities that may depend on preceding choices, and includes natural written languages among the examples.

evidence.support: Defines symbol-by-symbol discrete sources as stochastic processes and lists written languages.

limitations: This is a source model inside a communication theory, not a trained neural language model.

claims.statement: The paper specifies next-letter transition probabilities and then generalizes the construction to trigram and n-gram probabilities with progressively longer preceding contexts.

evidence.support: Defines transition, digram, trigram, and general n-gram probabilities.

limitations: The paper’s approximation order names differ from some modern Markov-order conventions.

claims.statement: Shannon constructs letter- and word-level English approximations ranging from independent sampling to digram-, trigram-, and word-transition-conditioned samples.

locator.label: Definitions on BSTJ p. 387 and §3 examples on p. 388
locator.section: 3. The Series of Approximations to English

evidence.support: Shows the explicit sequence of zero-, higher-order letter, and word approximations.

limitations: The examples are qualitative illustrations and do not test semantic understanding.

claims.statement: Under stated continuity, equiprobable-choice, and grouping assumptions, Shannon obtains the form H = -K Σ p log p and calls it the entropy of a probability distribution.

evidence.support: States the assumptions, the entropy form, and its interpretation as information, choice, or uncertainty.

limitations: Shannon presents the characterization theorem as plausibility for later definitions, not as a prerequisite for the rest of the theory.

claims.statement: The paper defines conditional entropy as average remaining uncertainty and states that knowing another event cannot increase that uncertainty.

evidence.support: Defines Hx(y), gives the joint-entropy decomposition, and compares conditional with unconditional uncertainty.

limitations: Conditional entropy here is not itself the modern neural cross-entropy training objective.

claims.statement: For the stationary-source treatment, Shannon defines F_N as the entropy of the Nth symbol conditioned on the preceding N−1 symbols and shows that F_N is non-increasing with N and approaches the source entropy.

locator.label: §7, Theorems 5–6 and discussion, BSTJ p. 398
locator.section: 7. The Entropy of an Information Source

evidence.support: Defines F_N using N−1 preceding symbols, proves its monotonic behavior, and relates its limit to source entropy.

limitations: The paper’s N counts the predicted symbol, so the conditioning context has length N−1.

claims.statement: The 1951 method studies how well a next letter can be predicted when preceding text is known and combines experimental prediction with theoretical analysis to bound entropy.

evidence.support: States the next-letter task, preceding-context condition, and upper/lower-bound purpose.

limitations: The task concerns characters in printed English, not word tokens.

claims.statement: In the prediction protocol, a subject guesses the next character of an unfamiliar passage and receives the correct character before proceeding, so later guesses always use the correct prefix.

evidence.support: Describes both the single-guess and repeated-guess versions and the feedback supplied after errors.

limitations: Prediction errors do not propagate into subsequent context.
The repeated-guess rank result must not be conflated with the separate single-guess illustration.

claims.statement: The main context experiment uses 100 randomly selected 15-character samples from Jefferson the Virginian, tests conditions with up to 14 preceding characters plus a reported long-context condition, and permits specified frequency tables, word lists, and a dictionary.

evidence.support: Describes the sampling frame, context conditions, source book, and aids available to the subject.

limitations: The first two Table I columns are calculated from frequency tables rather than observed in the human experiment.
The paper’s N and N-1 labels and its long-context column should be preserved rather than silently normalized.

claims.statement: Shannon reports that, apart from statistical fluctuations, the Table I predictions generally improve as the subject receives more knowledge of the preceding text.

evidence.support: States the observed context-versus-prediction trend and explicitly qualifies it for statistical fluctuation.

limitations: This trend is reported for the paper’s small human-prediction experiment and should not be generalized without its sampling caveat.

claims.statement: For the theoretical ideal predictor, possible next letters are ordered by decreasing true conditional probability given the preceding context, and the observed letter is represented by its rank.

evidence.support: Defines optimal conditional-probability ordering and rank encoding for the ideal predictor.

limitations: The ideal predictor is a theoretical oracle-like construction, not a trained machine.

claims.statement: The frequencies of correct-letter prediction ranks are used to derive upper and lower bounds on N-gram conditional entropy.

evidence.support: Gives the inequality connecting rank frequencies with upper and lower entropy bounds.

limitations: The lower-bound derivation assumes ideal conditional-probability ranking.

claims.statement: After smoothing the prediction-rank frequencies, Shannon reports approximately 0.6 to 1.3 bits per character for the long-context column of the experimental entropy bounds.

locator.label: §6, smoothed upper/lower bounds table, printed p. 64
locator.table: Unnumbered smoothed-bounds table derived from Table I

evidence.support: Reports the smoothed long-context upper and lower bounds and their units.

limitations: The result is conditional on the paper’s small, text-specific human-prediction procedure and smoothing choices.

claims.statement: Shannon warns that the long-context estimates have considerable sampling error and that human prediction does not exactly satisfy the ideal-predictor assumptions used in the lower-bound argument.

evidence.support: States the sampling-error and human-versus-ideal-prediction qualifications.

relationships.rationale: The works form a useful conceptual transition from measured symbol dependence in literary text to a general conditional stochastic-source framework. The reviewed sources do not document direct influence.

locator.label: Historical analysis, pp. 571–572
locator.paragraph: Comparison of Markov’s transmission and Shannon’s cited sources

sourceEvidence.support: Describes a transformed conceptual recurrence while noting that Shannon did not cite Markov, supporting a noncausal editorial bridge.

uncertainties.note: No direct citation or participant account connecting the Onegin study to Shannon’s paper has been identified.
uncertainties.resolutionCondition: Primary evidence would be required to promote this edge beyond editorial synthesis.

relationships.rationale: The 1951 paper explicitly cites the 1948 article for entropy and redundancy, then presents a more sensitive estimation method based on long-context predictability.

sourceEvidence.support: Names the previous paper, cites its two 1948 installments, and distinguishes the new prediction-based method.

learningObjectives.objective: Explain in plain language why different next-vowel rates after vowels and consonants show local dependence without turning the study into a modern language model.
learningObjectives.successCriterion: The learner can contrast independence with conditional frequency and name at least one limit of the binary abstraction.

learningObjectives.objective: Reconstruct the one- and two-predecessor conditional estimates and compare the reported dispersion values without claiming an exact Markov order or modern significance test.
learningObjectives.successCriterion: The learner can reproduce the conditional rates and state why the paper’s model-fit conclusion remains qualified.

learningObjectives.objective: Describe how adding local context changes the next-symbol choices and the visible structure of Shannon’s English approximations.
learningObjectives.successCriterion: The learner can distinguish independent sampling from a context-conditioned next-symbol choice and avoids equating resemblance with understanding.

learningObjectives.objective: Relate the discrete-source and n-gram constructions to conditional entropy F_N while preserving Shannon’s order convention and modeling assumptions.
learningObjectives.successCriterion: The learner can map a context length to its conditional distribution and explain why longer context cannot increase ideal conditional uncertainty.

learningObjectives.objective: Use the letter-guessing protocol to connect more informative preceding text with fewer plausible next characters and lower uncertainty.
learningObjectives.successCriterion: The learner can describe the feedback protocol and explain why guesses always use the correct prefix.

learningObjectives.objective: Separate human rank observations from the ideal conditional-probability predictor and trace how rank frequencies enter the reported entropy bounds.
learningObjectives.successCriterion: The learner can identify which bound depends on ideal ranking and enumerate the main sampling and protocol caveats.
